zones.name: .rex
insights: High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
These files are runtime artifacts, not source code — they should be excluded from static analysis and treated as data, not logic.
The execution-log.jsonl provides an audit trail of agent actions and is valuable for debugging hench runs.
Keeping config.json separate from prd.json allows configuration to be versioned independently of task state.
All four files are data/config artifacts with zero coupling to source code, which is correct — this zone should remain isolated and never become an import target.

zones.name: Cli
insights: 10 entry points — wide API surface, consider consolidating exports

The chunked-review family (chunked-review.ts, chunked-review-state.ts) suggests a sophisticated interactive workflow for reviewing large LLM outputs in batches — this is a meaningful UX pattern worth preserving as a named subsystem.

The adapter.ts command signals sync/integration capability with external systems (e.g. Notion or Linear); keeping it in the CLI zone rather than the store zone is correct since it's a command, not persistence logic.

Coupling of 0.49 with 54 imports from unit-analyze and 38 from core is high for an infrastructure zone — CLI commands should ideally be thin dispatch layers, delegating logic downward rather than importing broadly from domain zones.

CLI zone imports from unit-analyze (54) and core (38) while those zones also import back from cli — audit these cycles to ensure CLI commands are not being imported by domain logic, which would invert the dependency hierarchy.
mcp.ts colocated with CLI commands is appropriate since MCP is an alternative interface to the same commands, not a separate domain concern.
Zone "cli-infrastructure" has files across 9 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

store → cli (5 imports): the storage layer imports from the CLI zone, most likely to reuse output formatters or display utilities. This inverts the presentation/storage relationship — storage should be consumed by CLI, not the reverse. Any shared display utilities belong in a dedicated output/formatting module below both.

store → cli-infrastructure (5 imports): the persistence layer depends on the presentation layer. This is a direct layering inversion — if cli-infrastructure were replaced or refactored, store would break. Shared output utilities should be extracted to a lower-level module imported by both.
src/analyze/reshape-reason.ts has an src/analyze/ path prefix matching the analyze domain, yet is grouped in cli-infrastructure — its directory affinity is to prd-analyze-core, not the CLI layer.

src/cli/commands/constants.ts and src/cli/output.ts are each imported by 7 zones, making them effectively global — utilities at this breadth are foundation-level artifacts, but their residence in a CLI command zone forces every non-CLI consumer (store, analyze, schema) to take a CLI-layer dependency solely to access constants or output helpers.

src/analyze/reshape-reason.ts resides in src/analyze/ but is classified in cli-infrastructure. Its directory, name, and subject matter (analyze output reshaping) match prd-analyze-core. Misclassification means analyze logic is hidden inside the CLI zone and changes to analyze behavior require navigating CLI file listings.

src/cli/commands/constants.ts and src/cli/output.ts are both imported by 7 zones — every major zone in the package — but live inside the CLI command zone. Non-CLI zones (store, analyze, schema) must incur a CLI-layer dependency to use these utilities. Extracting them to a shared foundation module would break the forced CLI coupling for all downstream consumers.

cli-infrastructure and analyze-recommend share nearly identical structural fingerprints (cohesion/coupling 0.51/0.49 vs 0.5/0.5). When two zones in semantically unrelated domains converge on the same structural profile, it typically indicates zone boundaries were drawn by graph connectivity density rather than semantic ownership. Identical scores across different domains should be treated as a signal that at least one boundary is misdrawn rather than a confirmation that both zones are equally healthy.
Zone "cli" has files across 9 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory
[call graph] 1382 internal calls, 207 outgoing, 305 incoming (cohesion: 0.87, coupling: 0.13)

zones.name: Core
insights: 23 entry points — wide API surface, consider consolidating exports

At 47 files and 23 entry points this is the largest non-analyze zone; it risks becoming a catch-all — periodically audit whether subsets (e.g., sync-engine + sync) have grown cohesive enough to extract.
The balanced cohesion/coupling (0.51/0.49) is healthy for a domain core; it absorbs stable internal dependencies without over-exporting to peers.
Heavy inbound imports from unit-analyze (67) and cli (38) confirm this zone is the true domain foundation — keeping it import-free from those layers is architecturally correct.
23 declared entry points is unusually high; consider auditing which are truly public API versus internal cross-file imports that leaked into the entry-point list.
sync-engine.ts and sync.ts alongside canonical.ts suggest sync-related logic is partially here and partially in the store zone — verify ownership is intentional and not an accidental split.
public.ts is an entry point in this zone, correctly placing the package's public API surface at the domain core layer.

The 2 imports from prd-core-operations INTO task-selection (core→task: 2 crossings) are architecturally suspicious — core domain logic should not depend on a feature-level selection module; these 2 imports likely represent a shared concept (e.g. task scoring type) that belongs lower in the hierarchy.

prd-core-operations imports from task-selection (2 crossings) — a domain core importing from a feature module inverts the expected dependency direction; extract the shared concept into prd-core-operations or a shared type module.

The 23 declared entry points span types, utilities, analytics, sync, health, and keyword logic — a breadth suggesting this zone functions as the package's default dumping ground for any logic that doesn't obviously belong elsewhere. The absence of a forcing function (no sub-package boundary, no explicit access control) means the zone will continue to accumulate files as features are added.

prd-core-operations has 23 entry points across fundamentally different concerns (analytics, sync, health, keywords, code-coverage, DAG). Unlike a well-defined public API, this breadth means any change to the zone's internals potentially breaks consumers across all 23 surfaces. A versioned public.ts facade that re-exports only intentionally public symbols would contain blast radius.

analytics.ts and code-coverage.ts both read from the .hench/ run history directory — they depend on a directory produced by a sibling package (hench) through filesystem path convention rather than through a gateway import. This creates a hidden cross-package dependency that bypasses the gateway pattern documented in CLAUDE.md and will silently break if hench changes its run directory structure or output format.

sync-engine.ts, sync.ts, and canonical.ts together form a nascent sync subsystem within prd-core-operations that shares a clear data flow (canonical serialization → sync engine → sync command) distinct from the analytics, health, keywords, and DAG subgroups also in this zone. All three files couple primarily to prd-storage-integrations. Extracting them into a dedicated prd-sync zone is the lowest-risk reduction available because no new cross-zone edges would be created — the coupling is already targeted.

analytics.ts and code-coverage.ts read from .hench/ run history via filesystem path rather than a gateway import — this is an undocumented cross-package dependency on hench's directory layout. Add a typed path constant or minimal gateway to formalize this contract before hench restructures its output.

Extract sync-engine.ts + sync.ts + canonical.ts into a dedicated prd-sync zone. These three files share a serialization→engine→command data flow and couple primarily to prd-storage-integrations, making this the cleanest available split from the 47-file mega-zone with no new coupling introduced.
[call graph] 1591 internal calls, 77 outgoing, 128 incoming (cohesion: 0.95, coupling: 0.05)

zones.name: E2e
insights: Low cohesion (0.29) — files are loosely related, consider splitting this zone
High coupling (0.71) — 1 imports target "unit-analyze"
High coupling (0.71) — 1 imports target "prd-analyze-core"

src/schema/validate.ts is the sole production entry point — the community detection grouped it with e2e tests because the tests are its primary consumer, suggesting validate.ts has little integration with the runtime domain layers

The 8+ e2e test files covering cli-adapter, cli-analyze, cli-init, cli-prune, cli-recommend and others form a comprehensive integration harness; keeping them co-located with validate.ts makes cross-command schema regression easy to spot

Consider whether src/schema/validate.ts belongs closer to the PRD Core Operations or PRD Storage & Integrations zones at the domain level, since its current grouping is driven by test imports rather than production architecture
Cohesion is 0.29 — the zone is structurally fragmented because it blends a single production schema module with a broad e2e test suite; these have different change rates and concerns
Coupling is 0.71 — the e2e tests import heavily from CLI, core, and store zones, pulling the entire command surface into this zone's dependency footprint

src/schema/validate.ts is the entry point but is likely consumed by tests rather than other production modules; if true, it signals the validator is not yet wired into the runtime ingestion path (e.g. on prd.json load/save)

src/schema/validate.ts is architecturally orphaned: it is not imported by store, core, or any runtime path — meaning PRD data is loaded and saved without schema validation, creating a silent data-integrity gap

The e2e test files in this zone collectively import from at least 5 other zones (cli, core, store, unit-analyze, and prd-analyze-core), making schema-validation the de-facto cross-cutting e2e harness even though it has no formal role as a test infrastructure zone

validate.ts and src/schema/index.ts (a 7-zone hub) live in the same schema directory but are in different zones — their separation signals that validation was added after the schema foundation was stabilized and was never wired back in
src/schema/validate.ts has zero runtime consumers: store and core zones never import it, so PRD files are never validated on load or save — any malformed prd.json silently corrupts domain state

The e2e suite in this zone functions as a cross-package integration harness (touching cli, core, store) but is not named or isolated as such — test files spanning 5+ zones should live in a dedicated e2e zone or tests/e2e root, not co-located with a single production schema module

src/schema/validate.ts should be moved to the store or core zone and wired into the prd.json read/save path; its current zone placement is purely an artifact of Louvain grouping it with its test consumers, not architectural intent

src/schema/validate.ts is misplaced: it belongs adjacent to the store zone's prd.json load/save path, not co-located with e2e tests. Moving it enables a straightforward wiring of schema validation into the persistence boundary without changing any public API.

The zone name 'schema-validation' is a misnomer: 15 of 16 files are e2e tests, yet the zone is named after the single production file. Zone IDs serve as shared vocabulary in commit messages and reviews; a name implying a production concern misleads contributors about where to add new e2e tests or find the validation module.

schema-validation is the only zone in the package that simultaneously breaches both risk thresholds (cohesion < 0.5 AND coupling > 0.5), making it the highest-risk zone by the combined-indicator criterion — not merely a low-cohesion outlier.

The 8+ e2e test files follow a strict cli-{command}.test.ts naming convention (cli-adapter, cli-analyze, cli-import, cli-init, cli-prune, cli-quiet, cli-recommend). This convention is implicit and undocumented; a contributor adding a new CLI command has no documented signal that the matching e2e file belongs here rather than in tests/e2e/ or alongside the command file.

Rename the zone from 'schema-validation' to 'e2e' or 'tests-e2e' to reflect its actual composition (15/16 files are e2e tests). The current name is a naming inconsistency: it labels the zone after its minority production file rather than its dominant file type.

schema-validation has BOTH low cohesion (0.29) AND high coupling (0.71) — the explicit combined-risk pattern flagged in the severity guide. This zone is fragile: any change to the CLI surface, core, or store will ripple through the e2e tests here, and the misnamed zone makes the blast radius invisible to reviewers.

Document the cli-{command}.test.ts naming convention for e2e tests in a tests/e2e/README.md or CONTRIBUTING guide. Without it, new CLI commands will accumulate e2e tests in inconsistent locations, increasing the zone's already-high coupling further.
[call graph] 309 internal calls, 1 outgoing, 22 incoming (cohesion: 1, coupling: 0)

zones.name: Root
insights: High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
The presence of context.md and llms.txt at the root suggests sourcevision has been run on the rex package itself, producing AI-readable summaries alongside human-readable docs.
No entry points means this zone is purely declarative — build tooling and documentation only, which is the expected structure for a package root.
Rex-F.png and Rex.png are brand assets that could be moved to a dedicated docs/ or assets/ directory to keep the root clean.
context.md and llms.txt co-located with package.json at root is unconventional; consider relocating them to a .sourcevision/ subdirectory to match the tool's own output convention.

context.md and llms.txt in package-root are sourcevision output artifacts — when sourcevision is run on the rex package itself, these files enter the zone graph, creating a self-referential loop where analysis output is analyzed. This inflates the package-root zone file count and contaminates the architectural graph with tool artifacts distinct from any source file.

context.md and llms.txt are sourcevision output files appearing in the rex package-root zone. Add them to the sourcevision ignore list for the rex package to prevent self-referential zone contamination — tool output should not be indistinguishable from source files in the zone graph.

zones.name: Store
insights: 9 entry points — wide API surface, consider consolidating exports
The healthy cohesion/coupling balance (0.6/0.4) confirms this zone is well-bounded — storage concerns are cleanly separated from domain logic.
Integration schemas for Jira and Notion are co-located with the adapter registry, which is correct: schema definitions belong next to the adapters that consume them.
canonical.ts as a utility in this zone is a good fit — canonical serialization is fundamentally a storage-layer concern ensuring deterministic JSON round-trips.
Highest cohesion score in this batch (0.6) — this zone demonstrates well-scoped responsibility and is a model for how the larger zones could be organized.
5 imports from store→core and 10 from core→store indicate bidirectional coupling at the domain/storage boundary; verify this is intentional layering rather than a circular dependency.
Zone "prd-storage-integrations" has files across 8 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

prd-storage-integrations has zero coupling to prd-fix-command and task-selection zones — confirming storage is correctly isolated from all feature-specific domain logic in this batch; it only couples to cli, core, and unit-analyze as expected for a persistence layer.
Storage zone has no cross-zone edges to any feature command zone (fix, task-selection, usage) — a clean separation that prevents feature churn from destabilizing storage contracts.

The store zone's bidirectional coupling with core (store→core 5 imports, core→store 10 imports) means the domain core directly manages persistence details rather than depending on an abstract repository interface. This is the same SCC risk noted globally but manifests concretely here as store calling into core and core calling back into store — neither layer has a clean abstraction surface.

integration-schemas/ is the only subdirectory-namespaced sub-module in this batch — its index.ts acts as a mini-public-API for its schema definitions. This sub-module nesting pattern (named subdirectory + index.ts) is the correct internal organization strategy for complex zones, but is not applied to prd-core-operations despite that zone having at least three naturally grouped subsets (analytics/coverage, sync/canonical, health/keywords). The pattern exists as a working example one zone away from where it is most needed.

The adapter-registry pattern is unique in the package — no other zone implements a plugin registry. Its correctness guarantees are currently enforced only by convention: an adapter that partially implements contracts.ts will be accepted at registration time and fail at runtime. As new adapters (beyond Jira/Notion) are added, this deferred validation becomes an increasing source of hard-to-diagnose runtime errors.

Apply the integration-schemas/ sub-module pattern (named subdirectory + index.ts barrel) to prd-core-operations by grouping analytics.ts/code-coverage.ts, sync-engine.ts/sync.ts/canonical.ts, and health.ts/keywords.ts into named subdirectories. This imposes internal structure without requiring a zone split and mirrors the only working example of this pattern in the batch.

adapter-registry.ts should validate that a registered adapter fully implements all contracts.ts interface methods at registration time rather than deferring failures to runtime use. Fail-fast registration converts a hard-to-diagnose runtime error into an immediate, actionable error at adapter setup.

The store zone is the only domain zone in the package with no corresponding unit test zone. Four other domain zones have dedicated test zones (unit, unit-analyze, unit-cli, unit-core), but 'unit-store' does not exist. This structural absence in the zone topology independently corroborates finding 12's three-way safety gap without requiring LLM analysis.
Zone "store" has files across 8 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory
[call graph] 231 internal calls, 48 outgoing, 53 incoming (cohesion: 0.83, coupling: 0.17)

zones.name: Unit
insights: The mixed presence of production source files (acknowledge.ts, reconcile.ts, scanners.ts, recommend/*) alongside their unit tests reflects a tight feedback loop — keep this pairing intentional and resist splitting them apart.

The recommend CLI command (recommend.ts) is colocated here rather than in the broader CLI zone, suggesting it's tightly coupled to the recommendation domain logic rather than general CLI infrastructure.
Cohesion of 0.5 with coupling of 0.5 indicates this zone bridges two concerns (recommendation generation and PRD acknowledgement); consider whether these warrant separate zones as the feature grows.
Zone "analyze-recommend" has files across 6 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory
Cohesion (0.5) and coupling (0.5) are balanced but moderate — the zone spans recommendation creation and PRD reconciliation, which are related but distinct responsibilities.
9 imports from unit-analyze confirm this zone depends heavily on the core analyze machinery; the direction is correct (recommend depends on analyze, not vice versa).

The near-balanced bidirectional import ratio with prd-analyze-core (13 outbound : 9 inbound, ratio ≈ 1.4:1) is significantly more balanced than the cli↔prd-analyze-core ratio (54:30 ≈ 1.8:1), suggesting analyze-recommend wraps prd-analyze-core output rather than sharing primitives with it — a healthier relationship pattern.

analyze-recommend ↔ prd-analyze-core is the most balanced bidirectional pair in the graph; the near-equal import exchange (13 vs 9) indicates a cooperative wrapper relationship rather than a dependency inversion — this is the healthiest cross-zone coupling in the package.

The 9 reverse imports from prd-analyze-core back into this zone (unit-analyze→unit: 9) mean the core analyze pipeline takes a runtime dependency on the recommendation layer — the dependency arrow points against the expected hierarchy where recommendations consume analyze output, not the other way around.

If 'unit' in cross-zone imports maps to analyze-recommend, then prd-analyze-core and analyze-recommend form a mutual dependency pair that cannot be compiled or tested independently, eliminating the value of splitting them into separate zones.

prd-analyze-core imports 9 symbols back into analyze-recommend (unit-analyze→unit: 9): the core analyze pipeline depends on a higher-level recommendation wrapper. This inverts the intended flow (recommend wraps analyze) and means neither zone can be loaded without the other, collapsing their boundary at runtime.

src/cli/commands/recommend.ts carries a cli-command archetype but resides in the analyze-recommend domain zone rather than cli-infrastructure. Every other CLI command file is in cli-infrastructure; this single outlier breaks the convention that CLI dispatch files belong to the CLI zone. The inconsistency makes it unclear whether CLI commands are owned by their domain zone or by the CLI infrastructure zone — a policy that should be explicit and uniform.

analyze-recommend is the only zone whose name contains a verb ('recommend'). All other zone names are noun phrases describing a domain or structural role. Verb-named zones describe actions rather than ownership boundaries, making them harder to reason about as encapsulation units and harder to map to directory structures.

src/cli/commands/recommend.ts (archetype: cli-command) is the only CLI command file outside cli-infrastructure. Either move it to cli-infrastructure to match the convention that CLI dispatch lives in one zone, or document explicitly that CLI commands are colocated with their domain zone — whichever policy is chosen should be applied uniformly across all command files.
Zone "unit" has files across 6 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory
[call graph] 328 internal calls, 62 outgoing, 1 incoming (cohesion: 0.84, coupling: 0.16)

zones.name: Unit Analyze
insights: 23 entry points — wide API surface, consider consolidating exports

With 70 files and 23 entry points this is the largest and most central zone — it owns the schema (v1.ts, levels.ts, index.ts) as well as the full analyze pipeline, making it a high-leverage area where changes have broad impact.

The bidirectional imports with both the cli zone (30 imports in, 54 out) and the core zone (18 imports in, 67 out) indicate this zone is deeply embedded in the cross-package call graph; any refactor here cascades widely.
Cohesion of 0.55 is acceptable given the zone's breadth, but the schema types (src/schema/) could be extracted into a dedicated schema zone to reduce the surface area and improve cohesion of both.
The schema files (v1.ts, levels.ts, index.ts) are bundled with analyze pipeline logic — extracting them to a dedicated schema zone would improve cohesion and make the type contract more auditable.

Bidirectional coupling with cli (unit-analyze → cli: 30 imports; cli → unit-analyze: 54 imports) creates a tight circular dependency risk — verify that no analyze module imports a CLI command module directly.
Zone "prd-analyze-core" has files across 10 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

prd-analyze-core imports from store (7 imports: unit-analyze→store): analyze pipeline logic should return results to callers, not read or write storage directly — this is a separation-of-concerns violation that ties the analyze domain to the persistence layer.

prd-analyze-core is imported by every non-trivial zone (core, cli, store, unit, unit-core, e2e) while simultaneously importing back from cli, core, and store — it is structurally impossible to isolate, test, or replace this zone without touching the entire package.

prd-analyze-core → store (7 imports): the analyze pipeline accesses storage directly, creating a dependency from a pure domain layer to the persistence layer. Analyze logic should pass results to callers who decide what to persist — this is a layering inversion.

prd-analyze-core is the only zone imported by all six other non-trivial zones simultaneously while also importing back from three of them. Its de facto role as a foundation layer is structurally incompatible with its position as a mid-tier domain zone — it should either be explicitly promoted to the foundation tier or split so shared primitives live below it.

23 exported entry points with no enforced public/private boundary means consumers across all 6 importing zones routinely bind to internal helpers; any internal refactor is a de facto breaking change regardless of intent.

src/schema/index.ts living inside prd-analyze-core forces every zone that needs only schema types to take a compile-time dependency on the full analyze pipeline — type-only consumers are unnecessarily coupled to pipeline churn.

The 7 outbound imports to store (unit-analyze→store: 7) combined with 20 inbound from store (store→unit-analyze: 20) confirm a bidirectional persistence dependency: analyze logic both reads from and is consumed by the storage layer, eliminating any possibility of testing analyze logic without a real store.

src/schema/index.ts (hub: imported by 7 zones) is bundled inside prd-analyze-core alongside analyze pipeline logic. Schema types are structural contracts that belong in a foundation layer; colocating them with pipeline code forces all 7 consumers to take a transitive dependency on the analyze pipeline to access types.

23 public entry points with no enforced boundary between public API and internal helpers allow consumers to import arbitrarily deep into the zone's internals. This makes the effective API surface equal to the zone's full file tree, not its intended contract.

The zone has 23 declared entry points but the subpath exports wildcard (dist/*) means the effective API surface is all 70 files. The 23-entry-point count creates a false sense of governance: consumers see a number implying a controlled API but face no enforcement. This gap between declared and actual API surface is more misleading than having no declared entry points at all, because it suggests boundary discipline exists when none is enforced.

Replace the dist/* wildcard export in package.json with the 23 explicit named entry points so that any consumer importing an undeclared path receives a compile-time error rather than silently accessing internals. The declared-but-unenforced boundary is actively misleading — enforcement converts the advisory list into an actual contract.
Zone "unit-analyze" has files across 10 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory
[call graph] 2743 internal calls, 291 outgoing, 187 incoming (cohesion: 0.9, coupling: 0.1)

zones.name: Unit Cli
insights: High coupling (0.6) — 3 imports target "unit-analyze"
High coupling (0.6) — 3 imports target "prd-analyze-core"
Zone is minimal (4 files) and tightly scoped to a single command — good separation of CLI dispatch from core repair logic.
Cohesion sits exactly at the warning threshold; consider whether fix.ts core logic belongs in the larger core zone rather than isolated here.
The test files mirror the production split (cli vs core), suggesting the fix operation has meaningful complexity worth preserving as a unit.
Cohesion (0.4) and coupling (0.6) are both at the boundary thresholds; monitor if this zone grows, as low cohesion at small size often signals a split that should be consolidated.
Production source is split across two layers (cli/commands and core) in the same zone — the zone correctly captures a full vertical slice of one feature.

The 0.6 coupling score is inflated by zone size (4 files): in small zones, a few imports dominate the metric — the absolute import count (3) indicates narrow, focused dependency on prd-analyze-core, not broad fan-out.

prd-fix-command → prd-analyze-core coupling is strictly unidirectional (fix reads analyze output to repair PRD items); no reverse edge exists, making this one of the cleanest consumer relationships in the batch.
Unidirectional coupling to prd-analyze-core (fix consumes analyze output, never the reverse) — this is a well-defined downstream consumer with a clean, stable interface boundary.

src/core/fix.ts imports exclusively from ../schema/index.js and ./tree.js — both are foundational lower-layer modules. It has zero imports from cli, store, analyze, or any peer-domain module. This is the only core file in the batch with a fully clean downward-only dependency chain, making the previously suggested merge into prd-core-operations risky: absorption into a zone with known upward dependencies would likely contaminate fix.ts's clean boundary.

The CLI command file (src/cli/commands/fix.ts) contains ~100 lines of output formatting logic (outputResult, buildSummary, kindIcon, kindLabel) that could be extracted to a shared formatter, but these are self-contained within the zone and do not create cross-zone coupling — this is an internal cohesion observation, not an architectural violation.

fix.ts (CLI layer) correctly keeps output formatting functions (outputResult, buildSummary, kindIcon, kindLabel) inside the CLI command file rather than in the core module — this is the architecturally correct pattern, contrasting directly with token-usage-analytics where formatters were embedded in core. The divergence between these two zones' approaches to the same problem is undocumented; new contributors will follow whichever example they encounter first, with no signal about which is correct.

kindIcon and kindLabel are domain-to-display mappings for item kinds (epic, feature, task, subtask) — the same kind vocabulary appears wherever tree items are rendered in other command files. These helpers are private to fix.ts with no reuse path, meaning equivalent mappings will be silently duplicated in other command files as new kinds are added.

kindIcon/kindLabel in fix.ts map item kinds to display symbols — identical mappings are needed wherever tree items are rendered. Extract to output.ts or a shared kinds-display.ts to prevent silent duplication across command files as new item kinds are added.

Document that the correct pattern is to keep output formatting in the CLI command file (as fix.ts does), not in the core module. This policy diverges from token-usage-analytics and should be made explicit to prevent new code following the wrong precedent.
[call graph] 136 internal calls, 15 outgoing, 1 incoming (cohesion: 0.9, coupling: 0.1)

zones.name: Unit Core
insights: High coupling (0.59) — 7 imports target "unit-analyze"
High coupling (0.59) — 7 imports target "prd-analyze-core"
Five of seven files are tests, indicating strong coverage investment in task-selection correctness — this is appropriate given the algorithmic nature of scoring and prioritization.
The cohesion score (0.41) is near the warning floor; the CLI command file is the outlier pulling it down, since next-task core logic is internally cohesive.

Requirements-prioritization test living here rather than in the core zone hints that prioritization logic may straddle two zones — confirm it belongs with task selection rather than PRD core operations.
Cohesion (0.41) is just above the warning threshold; if the CLI command were moved to CLI Infrastructure, the remaining core + tests would likely score significantly higher.
7 imports into unit-analyze suggest task-selection logic depends on analyze output (e.g., proposal acceptance state), which is an expected and appropriate dependency direction.

The 7 imports task-selection → prd-analyze-core are strictly unidirectional (task scoring reads proposal/analyze state); this is a correct downstream consumption pattern with no reverse edge, making the analyze→task direction a clean zone interface.

The requirements-prioritization test file's co-location in task-selection while potentially depending on analyze primitives suggests the prioritization concept straddles zone boundaries at the test layer even if production code does not.
task-selection → prd-analyze-core (7 imports) is unidirectional — a clean consumer relationship where task scoring reads analyze output without any reverse dependency.

5 of 7 zone files are test files, leaving only next-task.ts (core logic) and next.ts (CLI command) as production files. With a 2-file production footprint, this zone is primarily a test zone masquerading as a feature zone — the zone boundary provides naming but offers no encapsulation that couldn't be achieved by moving next-task.ts into prd-core-operations and relying on filename scoping.

The task-selection zone has only 2 production source files (next-task.ts and next.ts) and 5 test files. At this size the zone adds organizational overhead without encapsulation benefit. Merging next-task.ts into prd-core-operations and next.ts into cli-infrastructure would eliminate an unnecessary zone boundary without any coupling regression.

requirements-prioritization.test.ts and feature-filtered-task.test.ts both test named concepts that have no corresponding dedicated production source files — these concepts are embedded within next-task.ts without explicit module separation. The test file names advertise a modular architecture that does not exist in production, making the zone's structure misleading about where logic actually lives.

The zone has 2 production files but 5 test files each targeting a distinct named concept (next-task, next-task-matching, next-task-scoring, requirements-prioritization, feature-filtered-task). The 5:2 ratio of named test concerns to named production modules indicates next-task.ts carries multiple implicitly separated responsibilities that are only visible through test file naming rather than through source structure.

requirements-prioritization.test.ts and feature-filtered-task.test.ts name concepts with no corresponding production module. Either create dedicated source files to match the test-implied architecture, or rename the test files to reflect that they test slices of next-task.ts behavior — the current mismatch misleads readers about production module boundaries.
[call graph] 444 internal calls, 24 outgoing, 4 incoming (cohesion: 0.95, coupling: 0.05)

zones.name: Usage
insights: Low cohesion (0.29) — files are loosely related, consider splitting this zone
High coupling (0.71) — 3 imports target "cli"
High coupling (0.71) — 3 imports target "cli-infrastructure"
Low cohesion (0.29) combined with high coupling (0.71) signals that this zone is poorly bounded — likely because token-usage logic has grown dependencies on many unrelated zones.
The zone is small (4 files) but its coupling score is the highest in this batch; this suggests token-usage.ts imports broadly across the codebase rather than using a narrow, stable interface.
Consider whether token tracking belongs as a thin utility consumed by core operations rather than a standalone zone with heavy external dependencies.
Cohesion 0.29 and coupling 0.71 both breach warning thresholds simultaneously — this is the weakest zone in the batch and warrants architectural review.
High coupling at small file count suggests the implementation reaches into many other zones; introduce a narrow interface or aggregate token data at a single collection point to reduce fan-out.
Regression tests are co-located with unit tests in the same zone, which is a positive signal that the token-usage logic has known edge cases being actively guarded.

The 3 imports from token-usage.ts INTO cli-infrastructure mean domain utility logic imports from the CLI presentation layer — this is a concrete layering violation (domain→presentation), not just a loose coupling score.

token-usage-analytics is the only zone in this batch where a domain core file (token-usage.ts) reaches upward into the CLI layer; all other core files in comparable zones import only from peer-domain or lower-layer zones.

token-usage.ts imports from cli-infrastructure (3 edges) — domain logic importing from the CLI presentation layer is a layering inversion; extract the needed CLI surface into a shared constant or inject it as a parameter to eliminate the upward dependency.

src/core/token-usage.ts has zero imports from cli-infrastructure: its only external imports are @n-dx/llm-client (PROJECT_DIRS) and ../schema/index.js. The previously noted layering inversion (domain→CLI) is a misattribution — the 3 cross-zone edges originate from src/cli/commands/usage.ts (a CLI command file inside the zone) importing cli-infrastructure constants and output helpers, which is expected CLI behavior.

preflightBudgetCheck (token-usage.ts:816) uses a dynamic import of ../store/index.js with an explicit comment 'Dynamic import to avoid circular dependency with store' — confirming a live runtime circular dependency that is deferred rather than resolved structurally.

formatAggregateTokenUsage and formatBudgetWarnings (token-usage.ts:579,793) are presentation functions producing emoji-decorated CLI strings (⚠ BUDGET EXCEEDED) embedded inside the domain core module rather than in the CLI command layer. This couples the core module to a specific display context and prevents reuse in non-terminal consumers such as the web dashboard without pulling in terminal-formatted strings.

token-usage.ts is 838 lines and combines six distinct responsibilities: data extraction from three sources, event normalization, command grouping, time-period bucketing, cost estimation, and CLI display formatting. This is the largest single-file utility in the batch by line count and violates single-responsibility at the file level.

preflightBudgetCheck uses a dynamic import to break a static circular dependency with the store module (line 820, explicit comment). Deferring a cycle to runtime rather than removing it leaves the dependency graph incorrect at the module-graph level and prevents tree-shaking. The function should be moved to a caller that already has a store reference, or the shared concept (config loading) extracted to a lower module.

token-usage.ts embeds CLI display formatting functions (formatAggregateTokenUsage, formatBudgetWarnings) that produce terminal-oriented strings with emoji directly in the domain core file. This is not a CLI→domain import violation, but it IS a presentation/domain mixing violation: the domain module is now coupled to a specific output format, breaking reusability outside CLI contexts.

src/cli/commands/usage.ts imports formatAggregateTokenUsage and formatBudgetWarnings directly from token-usage.ts, leaving the CLI command with no formatting agency — it cannot choose a different output format, suppress sections, or adapt for non-terminal contexts (CI output, JSON mode, web dashboard) without modifying the domain core. The command is structurally a pass-through that calls core formatting rather than a command that composes raw data with its own presentation logic.

token-usage.ts at ~838 lines is the largest single utility file in this batch by a significant margin. A per-file line count lint threshold (e.g., max 400 lines) would have flagged this file before it reached its current state and would prevent other core utilities from following the same growth pattern without a deliberate policy decision.

Move formatAggregateTokenUsage and formatBudgetWarnings from token-usage.ts into usage.ts (CLI command). token-usage.ts should expose raw data structures; the CLI command composes those structures with its own display logic. This is the concrete before/after refactor that resolves both the presentation/domain mixing and the web-reuse blockage in a single move.

Add a per-file line count check to CI or lint config with a threshold around 400 lines. token-usage.ts at ~838 lines is the motivating case; the rule would also catch future accumulation in analytics.ts or other large utilities before they reach the same complexity ceiling.

The usage zone's sub-minimum file count (4) and lowest cohesion (0.29) are consistent with a zone that was split off mechanically by the Louvain algorithm rather than by deliberate design. In the absence of a zone archetype policy (finding 7), this outcome is expected and will recur for any small, loosely connected cluster. The fix is policy-level (define minimum viable zone sizes), not zone-level.
Zone "usage" was identified from filename patterns, not directory structure — consider creating src/cli/commands/usage/ and moving related files there

The strongest cross-zone flow (core → unit-analyze: 67, cli → unit-analyze: 54) correctly points toward the analyze pipeline as the system's gravitational center — schema and analyze logic are the most shared contracts.

Bidirectional imports between cli ↔ unit-analyze and cli ↔ core suggest partial inversion of the intended dependency hierarchy; a strict layering rule (CLI imports domain, domain never imports CLI) should be enforced and verified.

The usage and e2e zones (cohesion 0.29) fall well below the warning threshold — these are likely thin integration zones by design, but their low cohesion should be revisited to confirm files are correctly grouped.

The store zone (cohesion 0.6, coupling balanced across core/cli/unit-analyze) plays a healthy hub role for persistence without becoming a god module — its coupling is distributed rather than concentrated.

The core zone (47 files, 23 entry points) absorbs disproportionate complexity — the next natural refactoring step would be extracting sync-engine+sync into a dedicated sync zone, given the store zone already owns the adapter side of sync.

Bidirectional imports between core↔unit-analyze (67+18) and core↔cli (38+15) indicate these three zones form a tightly coupled triangle; this is the highest architectural risk in the package and worth monitoring as a potential merge or explicit layering decision.
token-usage-analytics is an outlier in zone health; all other zones in this batch sit at acceptable cohesion/coupling ratios, making token-usage a priority candidate for refactoring.

The cross-zone import graph shows a dense bidirectional cycle among core, cli, unit-analyze, and store (core→unit-analyze 67, cli→unit-analyze 54, unit-analyze→cli 30, core→cli 15, cli→core 38) — this mutual dependency between what should be layered concerns (CLI dispatch vs. domain logic) is the single largest architectural risk in the codebase

unit-analyze acts as a hub with imports flowing in from core, cli, store, unit, and unit-core simultaneously; a zone that is imported by everything is a hidden global-state risk even if individual cohesion looks acceptable

store↔core and store↔cli both carry bidirectional edges, suggesting storage concerns have leaked into domain and presentation layers rather than being accessed only downward through a clean repository interface

Multiple bidirectional import relationships (cli ↔ unit-analyze, cli ↔ core, unit-analyze ↔ core) suggest the four-tier dependency hierarchy described in CLAUDE.md is not fully enforced at the rex package level — a dependency linting rule (e.g. eslint-plugin-import or a custom check) would prevent regressions.

The rex-runtime-state zone (.rex/) being detected as a zone is an artifact of sourcevision scanning data directories — consider adding .rex/ to the sourcevision ignore list so runtime artifacts do not pollute the architectural graph.

Four of five zones contain both CLI command files and core logic files in the same zone — this pattern is consistent and intentional (vertical slicing by feature), but it means CLI Infrastructure and these feature zones will always share files across two source directories.

The store zone is the only zone in this batch with no test files, suggesting integration and e2e tests cover storage rather than unit tests — verify this is adequate given the adapter-registry complexity.

Cross-zone import counts reveal a hub-and-spoke topology centered on unit-analyze, which receives imports from every other zone; if unit-analyze is misnamed and actually contains shared schema/type definitions, it may need to be repositioned as a foundation layer.

Bidirectional imports between cli and core (cli→core 38, core→cli 15) violate the expected layering where CLI depends on domain but domain should not depend on CLI; this should be resolved by extracting the shared surface into a lower-level module

unit-analyze is imported by 6 distinct zones including cli, core, store, unit, unit-core, and e2e — its role as a universal dependency suggests it contains primitives that belong in a shared foundation layer rather than an analyze-specific module
The Rex Runtime State and Package Root zones both achieve cohesion of 1.0, indicating clean, well-bounded modules with no unnecessary coupling — a positive structural baseline for the package
src/analyze/ contains files from 3 zones (analyze-recommend, cli-infrastructure, prd-analyze-core) — consider grouping into subdirectories to clarify architectural boundaries

The core↔cli↔prd-analyze-core triangle (core→unit-analyze 67, cli→unit-analyze 54, unit-analyze→cli 30, unit-analyze→core 18, core→cli 15, cli→core 38) forms a fully-connected mutual dependency graph. No zone in this triangle can be compiled, tested, or replaced independently — they are architecturally a single merged module despite being split across three zone identifiers.

store participates in bidirectional coupling with all three triangle members (core↔store, cli↔store, unit-analyze↔store), expanding the strongly-connected component to four zones. The entire package core is one undifferentiated dependency cluster.

rex-runtime-state and package-root both achieve coupling: 0 and form a clean static boundary at the package periphery. These two zones demonstrate what healthy zone isolation looks like and serve as a baseline against which the triangle's coupling should be measured.

The only clean directed (non-bidirectional) import flows in the package are: unit-core→unit-analyze (7, inbound only) and e2e zone (no return edges listed). These two zones are the only consumers that do not create circular feedback — they represent the pattern all zones should follow.

unit-analyze→unit (9 imports in both directions: unit→unit-analyze 13, unit-analyze→unit 9): if 'unit' refers to analyze-recommend, then the test/recommendation layer and the core analyze layer are mutually dependent, preventing independent test execution. If 'unit' is a test zone, production code importing test utilities is a critical boundary violation.

The detected circular dependency (reason.ts ↔ extract.ts, and a 3-node variant including file-validation.ts) is entirely contained within the unit-analyze zone — it does not cross zone boundaries, which is structurally better than a cross-zone cycle, but still introduces file-level coupling that prevents safe independent modification of these three analyze files.

prd-fix-command and task-selection both independently target prd-analyze-core as their sole primary dependency without any coupling to each other — prd-analyze-core acts as a shared foundation hub for all feature command zones, a hub-and-spoke pattern where analyze is the spoke center.

All feature command zones (fix, task-selection, usage) have coupling scores ≥0.59 pointing primarily at unit-analyze or cli-infrastructure rather than at each other — horizontal isolation between feature zones is strong; the architectural risk is vertical (domain↔CLI and domain↔analyze layering), not lateral.

token-usage-analytics is the only zone with a domain→CLI import direction; every other zone with CLI coupling shows the expected direction (CLI imports domain). This makes token-usage-analytics a structural outlier, not just a metrics outlier.

The gap between src/schema/index.ts (hub, 7 importers, in the core zone) and src/schema/validate.ts (orphan, 0 runtime importers, in schema-validation zone) represents a split ownership problem: schema shape is universally shared but schema correctness is enforced nowhere at runtime

The e2e tests aggregated in the schema-validation zone collectively reach across every major zone boundary — this makes the schema-validation zone the highest fan-in test zone in the package, a property that is invisible from cohesion/coupling metrics alone

A strict read of the four-tier hierarchy (Orchestration → Execution → Domain → Foundation) has no designated place for cross-cutting e2e tests; their gravitational pull toward whichever production file they first tested (validate.ts) produces the misleading low-cohesion zones observed in schema-validation and likely usage as well — a tests/e2e top-level directory outside the zone graph would resolve both

The combination of validate.ts being runtime-orphaned and the store zone having no unit tests (only e2e coverage) means the critical prd.json read/write path has no schema enforcement and no unit-level regression net — these two gaps compound each other

Four zones (prd-analyze-core, cli-infrastructure, core, store) form a strongly-connected component where every zone imports from at least two others in the group. This is equivalent to a single monolithic module — the zone decomposition provides naming but no actual encapsulation boundary.

The package has two distinct structural tiers: an isolated periphery (rex-runtime-state, package-root with coupling 0) and a tightly-coupled core (the four-zone SCC). There is no intermediate tier — any new zone will either be fully isolated or immediately entangled. Establishing one intermediary layer with strict one-way import rules would break the current all-or-nothing topology.

The circular dependency among reason.ts, extract.ts, and file-validation.ts is zone-contained (all within unit-analyze) — resolve by extracting shared types to a separate module so each file can import types without creating a cycle.

prd-analyze-core is the dependency target for 3 distinct feature zones (fix: 3 imports, task-selection: 7 imports, token-usage: 4 imports) with no reverse edges — it acts as a stable foundation hub; consider formalizing its public API surface to protect downstream consumers from internal churn.
Runtime schema validation is absent: src/schema/validate.ts exists but is never called by store or core at load/save time, leaving the entire PRD data pipeline unguarded against malformed input

E2e test files are distributed across domain-named zones (schema-validation, unit-analyze) rather than isolated in a dedicated test infrastructure zone, causing cohesion scores to misrepresent production module quality and making it hard to reason about the true production dependency graph

No zone enforces a clean one-way interface to prd-analyze-core: it both exports to and imports from its consumers (cli, core, store). A facade or ports-and-adapters boundary around prd-analyze-core's outbound dependencies (especially to cli and store) would immediately reduce the SCC to two zones, which is the minimum intervention needed.

Horizontal zone-to-zone coupling between feature command zones (fix, task-selection, token-usage) is zero — feature zones are correctly isolated from each other and interact only through shared foundation layers, which is the intended architecture.

The schema-validation and store zones share a structural blind spot: store has no unit tests and validate.ts has no runtime callers — together they eliminate both the preventive (validation) and detective (unit tests) safety layers for the persistence boundary

A second circular dependency exists in the sourcevision package: packages/sourcevision/src/analyzers/enrich.ts ↔ packages/sourcevision/src/analyzers/zones.ts form a mutual import cycle. Previous insights only captured the rex package circular (reason.ts ↔ extract.ts). This cross-package occurrence indicates the circular pattern is not isolated to rex.

The three universal hub files (constants.ts, output.ts, schema/index.ts — each imported by 7 zones) all reside in mid-tier zones (cli-infrastructure or prd-analyze-core), not in a dedicated foundation layer. This is the structural root cause of the dense bidirectional coupling: consumers that need only a type or a constant must import an entire mid-tier zone, pulling its transitive dependencies along and creating artificial cycles.

The package has no mechanism to distinguish a zone's public API from its internal implementation: the subpath exports wildcard ('dist/*') allows any file in any zone to be imported directly. Introducing explicit public entry points per zone (one index.ts per zone boundary) is the minimum change needed to make the 23-entry-point problem in prd-analyze-core enforceable rather than advisory.

The five detected circular dependencies split cleanly by package: three in rex (reason.ts ↔ extract.ts, with file-validation.ts variant) and two in sourcevision (enrich.ts ↔ zones.ts). Both circulars are intra-zone (zone-contained), which limits blast radius but still prevents safe isolated compilation of the files involved.

The previously documented critical finding — 'token-usage.ts imports from cli-infrastructure (3 edges), a domain→CLI layering inversion' — is contradicted by the source code. token-usage.ts has no cli-infrastructure imports; the 3 edges originate from the CLI command file (usage.ts) inside the zone consuming cli-infrastructure constants and output helpers, which is architecturally correct. The misattribution inflates the severity assessment for this zone.

The dynamic import workaround in token-usage.ts (line 820) is the only confirmed runtime circular dependency in the package outside the reason.ts/extract.ts cycle in unit-analyze. Two confirmed runtime cycles in a package this size indicates that the static dependency inversion rule (domain should not import CLI or store) is not enforced by tooling and will produce additional cycles as the codebase grows.

src/cli/commands/usage.ts imports formatAggregateTokenUsage and formatBudgetWarnings directly from the domain core (token-usage.ts). This is the correct import direction (CLI imports domain), but it means the CLI command has no opportunity to adapt the output format — it is bound to whatever string layout the domain module chose to hardcode. A formatting interface injected at the call site would decouple display decisions from computation.

The three cleanest files in the batch by dependency hygiene are src/core/fix.ts (imports only schema + tree), src/core/token-usage.ts (imports only llm-client + schema, with one deferred store cycle), and the store/contracts.ts interface file (zero outbound runtime imports). These three represent the target state for all core files and serve as concrete positive examples for a dependency linting rule.

cli→store direct imports (20 total) allow CLI command files to read and write prd.json without passing through any domain validation. Combined with the confirmed absence of runtime schema validation on store load/save, this means a malformed PRD written by a CLI command bypasses both the preventive (schema check) and detective (unit test) layers simultaneously — the same compounding gap noted for store+validate, but now with an additional entry path.

The package has 5 zones with ≤9 files (.rex=4, unit-cli=4, usage=4, unit-core=7, root=9) while the largest zone has 70 files — a 17.5× size spread. This bimodal distribution (micro-zones + a mega-zone) indicates zone boundaries were not drawn at consistent granularity; a minimum viable zone size of ~10 files would collapse 3 peripheral zones and improve the size distribution.

Four zones share a 'unit-' prefix (unit, unit-analyze, unit-cli, unit-core) in a codebase where tests/unit/ is the unit test directory. This namespace collision means zone IDs can no longer be read as self-evident architectural labels without consulting the zone description — a property that degrades the value of zone-based reasoning for new contributors.

The zone renaming from descriptive domain names (prd-analyze-core, cli-infrastructure, prd-core-operations, prd-storage-integrations) to abbreviated generic names (unit-analyze, cli, core, store) reduces the self-documenting quality of the architectural graph. Zone IDs serve as shared vocabulary in commit messages, code review, and documentation; losing domain-specific names means losing the conceptual map embedded in the original naming.

The four micro-zones (unit-cli, usage, unit-core, .rex) collectively represent 19 files — fewer than the unit zone alone (14 files) and less than 10% of unit-analyze (70 files). These zones consume naming and maintenance overhead disproportionate to their encapsulation contribution; consolidating them would reduce zone count from 11 to 7-8 without losing meaningful architectural boundaries.

sourcevision/src/analyzers/enrich.ts ↔ zones.ts form a circular import pair (two cycles detected). This is a new circular not captured in prior insights and is separate from the rex reason.ts ↔ extract.ts cycle. Both files are in the sourcevision analyze pipeline; extracting shared types to a third module (e.g., zone-types.ts) would break both cycles simultaneously.

No production source zone achieves coupling: 0 — only data/config artifact zones (rex-runtime-state, package-root) are fully isolated. The absence of any production zone with zero coupling means there is no stable, independently deployable module in the entire codebase; all production logic forms a single strongly-connected component at the zone graph level.

The subpath exports wildcard ('"./dist/*": "./dist/*"') in package.json bypasses any zone boundary enforcement: consumers can import any internal file directly, making zone cohesion metrics descriptive but not enforceable. Replacing the wildcard with explicit named exports per zone would convert architectural observations into compile-time guarantees.

A confirmed runtime circular dependency exists between token-usage.ts and store/index.ts, worked around via dynamic import. Combined with the file-level cycle in unit-analyze (reason.ts ↔ extract.ts ↔ file-validation.ts), the package has at least two active runtime cycles. Neither is caught by static analysis because one is zone-contained and one uses dynamic import. A dependency graph lint (e.g. eslint-plugin-import/no-cycle) run in CI would surface both and prevent new cycles from being introduced silently.

Presentation logic (formatAggregateTokenUsage, formatBudgetWarnings) is embedded in src/core/token-usage.ts rather than in the CLI command layer. This is not a layering inversion by import direction, but it is a responsibility inversion by concern: the domain module owns how its data is displayed rather than exposing raw data for the consumer to format. Any non-CLI consumer (web dashboard, JSON API) must either use the CLI-formatted strings or duplicate the computation.

Zone ID namespace pollution: four zones share the 'unit-' prefix (unit, unit-analyze, unit-cli, unit-core) while the test directory is also named tests/unit/. Any tooling, documentation, or grep pattern using 'unit' as a qualifier will produce ambiguous results. Adopt a naming convention that separates test infrastructure zones from domain feature zones (e.g., use domain nouns for production zones, explicit 'tests-' prefix for test-only zones).

Five zones have ≤9 files each, creating a bimodal size distribution (micro-zones vs 70-file hub). Below ~8 files, zone boundaries provide no practical encapsulation and only add graph complexity. Establish a zone merge policy: any zone with fewer than 8 files that is not architecturally isolated (coupling > 0, no clear ownership) should be merged into its primary dependency or consumer.

The cross-zone import table uses abbreviated zone aliases (unit-analyze, cli, core, store, unit) that do not match canonical zone IDs (prd-analyze-core, cli-infrastructure, prd-core-operations, prd-storage-integrations, analyze-recommend). The alias 'unit' is entirely unresolvable without a side-channel mapping table — no zone in the full list is named 'unit', so tracing the 13-import and 9-import edges attributed to it requires manual cross-referencing. An import graph that cannot be read without a separate translation layer provides weaker architectural signal than no graph at all.

No production source zone achieves cohesion above 0.6 (store is the ceiling at exactly 0.6; all others range 0.29–0.55). This uniform moderate-cohesion ceiling across all production zones indicates zone boundaries are drawn at a granularity that mixes concerns by design rather than by accident. Splitting each zone at its natural seam — CLI dispatch from domain logic, schema types from pipeline logic — would likely push cohesion above 0.7 for the resulting sub-zones and is the structural prerequisite for any meaningful coupling reduction.

Every cross-zone import edge in the package is bidirectional — there are no strictly one-way dependencies among production zones. A zone graph with zero directed acyclic paths is functionally equivalent to a single monolithic module; zone boundaries provide naming and file organization but zero encapsulation. The minimum intervention to create even one true directed layer is extracting the three universal hub files (constants.ts, output.ts, schema/index.ts) into a foundation zone with no outbound runtime imports — this single move would break at least three bidirectional cycles simultaneously and establish the first enforceable dependency direction in the graph.

The package exhibits a two-class zone taxonomy with no intermediate tier: data artifact zones at cohesion=1/coupling=0 and production source zones clustered at 0.29–0.6. The absence of any intermediate high-cohesion/low-coupling zone means there is no stable, independently testable module between the pure data artifacts and the tightly coupled production cluster. The three hub files have the structural profile to form such an intermediate zone — their current residence in mid-tier zones is what collapses the intermediate tier and forces the binary topology.

The 'prd-' prefix is applied to 3 of 5 zones in this batch (prd-fix-command, prd-core-operations, prd-storage-integrations) but absent from task-selection and token-usage-analytics. No documented zone naming policy governs prefix usage; inconsistency will compound as zones are added. A naming policy decision — either all feature zones carry 'prd-' or none do — is needed to restore a consistent architectural vocabulary.

All five zones span both src/cli/commands/ and src/core/ directories (vertical slicing by feature) except prd-core-operations, which is a horizontal platform layer with no single CLI command counterpart. Vertical slices and horizontal layers require different design rules (slice: one CLI file + one core file + tests; layer: grouped by technical concern with subdirectory index files). Treating prd-core-operations as a vertical slice has caused it to absorb all non-slice logic by default, growing it to 47 files.

No zone in this batch has a dedicated zone-level index.ts that serves as its public API boundary. All exports reach consumers through package-level public.ts or the dist/* wildcard. Without per-zone index files, zone boundary changes have no compile-time enforcement — a file can be moved between zones with zero import errors, making zone membership invisible to the type checker and zone metrics advisory-only.

prd-fix-command correctly keeps output formatters in the CLI layer; token-usage-analytics incorrectly keeps them in the core layer. Both patterns exist simultaneously in the same package with no policy to adjudicate them. A new contributor writing a CLI command will encounter both examples with no signal about which is correct — this is a concrete onboarding risk resolvable by a single architectural decision record or lint rule.

The five zones in this batch represent five structurally distinct archetypes: minimal vertical slice (fix, 4 files), mega horizontal layer (core-ops, 47 files), test-dominated vertical slice (task-selection, 5/7 test files), storage platform (storage, 23 files), and analytics outlier (token-usage, both metrics below threshold). Five incompatible archetypes in a single package indicates zone design has been reactive rather than policy-driven; establishing 2-3 canonical zone archetypes with documented file-count ranges and cohesion targets would prevent the next accidental mega-zone.

The two zones with the worst zone-health metrics (schema-validation: cohesion 0.29/coupling 0.71, usage: cohesion 0.29) both contain e2e or integration test files co-located with production code. Test-production co-location is the structural root cause of poor cohesion scores package-wide — the Louvain algorithm groups files by import edges, so tests that import many zones drag their sole production co-resident into a high-coupling position regardless of that file's own dependency hygiene.

There is no zone in the package whose ID uses a 'tests-' or 'e2e-' prefix to signal test-only content. Test files are distributed across domain-named zones (schema-validation, unit-analyze, unit, unit-cli, unit-core), creating a systematic blind spot: zone cohesion metrics mix production and test change rates, making it impossible to distinguish a fragmented production module from a healthy production module with a large test suite by metrics alone.

The package has two structural tiers for test coverage: e2e tests co-located in schema-validation and unit tests scattered across unit-* zones — but no integration test zone. The store zone (no unit tests, only e2e coverage) and the validate.ts orphan together suggest integration-level tests (store + schema together) were never written, leaving a gap between the fine-grained unit tests and the coarse-grained CLI e2e harness.

The cross-zone import table uses short alias IDs (unit-analyze, cli, core, store, unit) that diverge from canonical zone IDs. The alias 'unit' cannot be resolved to any zone without manual lookup. Establish a documented canonical zone ID → alias mapping, or standardize one naming scheme across both zone definitions and import reports, so the dependency graph is self-contained and readable without side-channel knowledge.

Every cross-zone import edge is bidirectional — the production zone graph contains no directed acyclic paths. This makes all zone boundaries purely cosmetic with zero encapsulation value. The minimum structural fix is extracting the three universal hub files (constants.ts, output.ts, schema/index.ts) into a dedicated foundation zone with no outbound runtime imports, creating the first true directed layer and breaking at least three of the densest bidirectional cycles in a single operation.

sourcevision output artifacts (context.md, llms.txt) appear in the zone graph when sourcevision analyzes a package that has previously been analyzed. Add sourcevision output file patterns to a global ignore list so tool output is excluded from zone detection across all packages, preventing self-referential contamination of the architectural graph.

Establish and document a zone naming policy: decide whether the 'prd-' prefix is mandatory for domain feature zones or should be dropped in favor of plain domain nouns. Apply the decision retroactively to restore a self-documenting architectural graph where zone IDs encode their domain without consulting a description.

Introduce per-zone index.ts files as zone public API boundaries. Each zone re-exports only its intended public surface through its own index.ts; package-level public.ts selectively re-exports from zone indexes. This converts zone boundaries from advisory labels into compile-time constraints and makes over-broad export surfaces (like the 23-entry-point problem) immediately visible as oversized index files.

Adopt and document a formatter placement policy: presentation/display functions belong in CLI command files, not in domain core modules. The divergence between prd-fix-command (correct) and token-usage-analytics (incorrect) is the concrete before/after example. Enforce via an ADR or lint rule to prevent the incorrect pattern from becoming the new default.

Define canonical zone archetypes with expected file count ranges: (1) Feature Slice — 4-12 files, one CLI command + one core module + tests; (2) Platform Layer — 10-30 files, grouped by technical concern with subdirectory index.ts files; (3) Infrastructure Zone — 5-20 files, storage/sync/registry concerns. New zones should declare their archetype in a zone metadata comment, making structural deviation visible in code review.

Establish a naming convention that distinguishes test-only zones from production zones at the ID level (e.g., 'tests-e2e', 'tests-unit-analyze'). Currently four zones share the 'unit-' prefix and one zone ('schema-validation') is misnamed after its minority production file. A consistent prefix convention would make zone IDs self-documenting and allow tooling to exclude test zones from production coupling metrics.

The combination of (a) no runtime schema validation on store load/save, (b) no unit tests for the store zone, and (c) e2e tests for store-touching commands living in a misnamed zone means the prd.json persistence boundary has zero automated safety at any layer that is easily findable or runnable in isolation. This three-way gap should be treated as a single coordinated fix: wire validate.ts into store, add store unit tests, and move e2e tests to a named tests-e2e zone.

Three hub files—src/cli/commands/constants.ts (7-zone fan-out, finding 2), src/schema/index.ts (22-file fan-in, finding 4), and src/cli/output.ts (60-call coupling, finding 18)—are the shared root cause of both the fan-in hotspots and the systemic zone bidirectionality (finding 10). These files prevent any zone from acting as a true directed layer boundary. Extracting them into a dedicated foundation zone is the single highest-leverage structural intervention; it addresses findings 2, 4, 10, and 11 in one operation.

All naming and taxonomy problems (findings 3, 7, 8, 9, 13) share one root cause: no zone archetype policy was established when the package scaled past ~5 zones. Findings 7 and 8 propose complementary solutions (archetype file-count ranges and prefix conventions) that should be implemented together, not sequentially. Applying one without the other leaves the policy incomplete.

The god function in src/cli/index.ts (finding 17, 48 unique calls) and the misplaced formatter anti-pattern (finding 6) both signal an underdefined CLI-layer responsibility model. The god function is a structural symptom; the misplaced formatter is the concrete violation. Together they point to a missing 'CLI coordinator' pattern in which command files own all presentation logic and main() owns only routing and dispatch.

Finding 13 (alias IDs diverge from canonical zone IDs, 'unit' alias unresolvable) introduces partial data confidence risk for finding 10 ('every cross-zone import edge is bidirectional'). If alias resolution was incomplete, some directed edges may have been miscategorized. Finding 10's critical severity remains justified—finding 1 independently measures a concrete bidirectional pair (18+67 crossings) and 369 total crossings across 40 pairs is strong signal—but the 'every edge' claim should be verified against canonical zone IDs before committing to the full extraction effort.

Findings 6 (formatter in wrong module), 10 (no directed layer), and 11 (no per-zone index.ts) together describe the same missing architectural primitive: enforced public APIs at zone boundaries. Without zone-level index.ts contracts, placement decisions cannot be validated by the compiler, and policy violations are detectable only by manual review. The three findings should be addressed as one coordinated initiative: establish zone public APIs first; formatter placement enforcement and directed layering follow structurally.

Finding 18 labels src/cli/commands/prune.ts → src/cli/output.ts as 'tightly coupled' based on 60 cross-file calls. The coupling is unidirectional (command → shared output), which is the correct and expected pattern for CLI presentation dependencies. The heuristic label is misleading; no action is warranted. This finding should be treated as informational noise, not a design problem.
src/analyze/ contains files from 3 zones (cli, unit, unit-analyze) — consider grouping into subdirectories to clarify architectural boundaries

Finding 14 (sourcevision output files contaminating zone graph) and finding 13 (alias IDs diverging from canonical zone IDs) both reduce confidence in zone metric data quality. These are upstream data hygiene issues that, if unresolved, mean subsequent analysis passes may re-flag the same phantom coupling and aliasing problems. Fixing them—by adding sourcevision output patterns to a global ignore list and standardizing zone ID naming—is a prerequisite for any future analysis pass producing clean, actionable metrics.

The structural absence of a 'unit-store' zone—while unit-analyze, unit-cli, unit-core, and unit all exist—is a topology-level corroboration of finding 12's three-way safety gap. The store zone is the only domain zone with zero automated safety at an isolated, runnable layer. This observation is independent of LLM analysis and verifiable directly from the zone list.

Findings 1, 10, and 16 all point to bidirectionality as the core architectural risk, but at three different scopes: a single measured pair (finding 1), the systemic whole-graph claim (finding 10), and the two worst-offending individual zones (finding 16). The convergence of automated heuristic (finding 1), LLM analysis (finding 10), and threshold breach (finding 16) at the same structural problem—bidirectional zone coupling—is the strongest cross-method corroboration in the full finding set. Finding 10's critical severity is the most defensible of the three, because it targets the systemic cause rather than individual symptoms.

Findings 1, 10, and 16 all point to bidirectionality as the core architectural risk, but at three different scopes: a single measured pair (finding 1), the systemic whole-graph claim (finding 10), and the two worst-offending individual zones (finding 16).
